## Managerial history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leicester_City_F.C.&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Managerial history")]

Main article: [List of Leicester City F.C. managers](/wiki/List_of_Leicester_City_F.C._managers "List of Leicester City F.C. managers")

The club's current manager is [Gary Rowett](/wiki/Gary_Rowett "Gary Rowett"), who was appointed on 18 February 2026 until the end of the 2025–26 season.[[179]](#cite_note-180)

[Nigel Pearson](/wiki/Nigel_Pearson "Nigel Pearson") and [Peter Hodge](/wiki/Peter_Hodge "Peter Hodge") have both had two separate spells in charge of the club. [Dave Bassett](/wiki/Dave_Bassett "Dave Bassett") also had a second spell as caretaker manager after his first spell as permanent manager.[[180]](#cite_note-181) Up until [Peter Hodge](/wiki/Peter_Hodge "Peter Hodge") was hired after [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the club had no official manager. A nominal role of secretary/manager was employed, though the board and the selection committee took control of most team affairs. It was Hodge who instated a system at the club for the manager having complete control over player and staff recruitment, team selection, and tactics. Though Hodge was originally titled "secretary/manager" he has retrospectively been named as the club's first official "manager."[[181]](#cite_note-OFAF-182)

[Matt Gillies](/wiki/Matt_Gillies "Matt Gillies") is the club's longest-serving manager. Between 1958 and 1968, he led Leicester City to four major domestic cup finals including a first major trophy, as well as four top eight finishes in the First Division. Under Gillies, the club competed for the First Division and FA Cup double in the 1962–63 season.

## Records and statistics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leicester_City_F.C.&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Records and statistics")]

Main article: [List of Leicester City F.C. records and statistics](/wiki/List_of_Leicester_City_F.C._records_and_statistics "List of Leicester City F.C. records and statistics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/2b/Leicester_City_FC_League_Performance.svg/500px-Leicester_City_FC_League_Performance.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Leicester_City_FC_League_Performance.svg)

Historical league positions of Leicester City within the English Football League system.

[Graham Cross](/wiki/Graham_Cross "Graham Cross") holds the record for the most Leicester appearances, with the defender playing 600 games between 1960 and 1976, increased from 599 following the club's decision to incorporate the 1971 FA Charity Shield into official records.[[182]](#cite_note-Charity_Shield-183) However, [Adam Black](/wiki/Adam_Black_(footballer,_born_1898) "Adam Black (footballer, born 1898)") holds the record for the most appearances in the league with 528 matches between 1920 and 1935.[[183]](#cite_note-Most_Appearances-184)

Striker [Arthur Chandler](/wiki/Arthur_Chandler_(footballer) "Arthur Chandler (footballer)") is currently the club's all-time record goalscorer, scoring 273 times in his 12 years at the club. He also scored in 8 consecutive matches during the 1924–25 season.[[3]](#cite_note-OBAF-3) The most goals managed in a single season for the club is 44 by [Arthur Rowley](/wiki/Arthur_Rowley "Arthur Rowley"), in the 1956–57 season.[[3]](#cite_note-OBAF-3) The fastest goal in the club's history was scored in 9 seconds by [Matty Fryatt](/wiki/Matty_Fryatt "Matty Fryatt") against [Preston North End](/wiki/Preston_North_End_F.C. "Preston North End F.C.") in April 2006.[[184]](#cite_note-Miscellaneous_Records-185)

Striker [Jamie Vardy](/wiki/Jamie_Vardy "Jamie Vardy") broke the previous Premier League record of 11 goals in 11 consecutive matches by scoring 13 goals in 11 consecutive matches during the 2015–16 Premier League season.[[185]](#cite_note-186) Vardy's goal at Sunderland on 10 April 2016 saw him become the first Leicester player since Gary Lineker in 1984–85 to score 20 top flight goals for the club, having already become Leicester's highest Premier League scorer in a single season.[[186]](#cite_note-auto-187) Vardy finished the campaign with 23 goals and was awarded the [Premier League Golden Boot](/wiki/Premier_League_Golden_Boot "Premier League Golden Boot") for the season.[[187]](#cite_note-188)

The record transfer fee paid by Leicester for a player was in the region of £32-to-40 million for midfielder [Youri Tielemans](/wiki/Youri_Tielemans "Youri Tielemans") from [AS Monaco](/wiki/AS_Monaco "AS Monaco").[[188]](#cite_note-189) The highest transfer fee received for a Leicester player was approximately £80 million from [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C.") for [Harry Maguire](/wiki/Harry_Maguire "Harry Maguire"); at the time of the transfer this was the eleventh-highest-ever fee,[[189]](#cite_note-190) the highest-ever move between two English teams,[[190]](#cite_note-191) and the highest-ever fee for a defender.[[191]](#cite_note-192)

Leicester's record home attendance is 47,298, for a fifth-round [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") match against [Tottenham Hotspur](/wiki/Tottenham_Hotspur "Tottenham Hotspur") at [Filbert Street](/wiki/Filbert_Street "Filbert Street") in 1928.[[192]](#cite_note-193) The current record home attendance at the current stadium is 32,242, for a [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") match against [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C.") on 8 August 2015.[[193]](#cite_note-194) The highest-ever attendance for a non-competitive football match at the King Power Stadium stands at 32,188, for a pre-season friendly against [Real Madrid](/wiki/Real_Madrid_C.F. "Real Madrid C.F.") on 30 July 2011.[[194]](#cite_note-Highest_Ever_Attendance-195)

Leicester's highest ever league finish is first place in the Premier League in [2015–16](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Premier_League "2015–16 Premier League").[[29]](#cite_note-538_Rise-29) The club currently holds the all-time record for second tier titles with eight.[[109]](#cite_note-EFL2T-109)

Leicester's longest unbeaten run in league football to date came between 1 November 2008 and 7 March 2009. The team remained unbeaten for 23 games on their way to the [League One](/wiki/Football_League_One "Football League One") title.[[195]](#cite_note-Leicester_Mercury_2009-03-12-196) The club's longest run of consecutive victories in league football is currently nine, which the team achieved between 21 December 2013 and 1 February 2014 in the [Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship").

During the 2015–16 season, Leicester achieved many new club records in what *[The Daily Telegraph](/wiki/The_Daily_Telegraph "The Daily Telegraph")* described as "one of the most astonishing league titles of all-time".[[196]](#cite_note-197) They recorded the most consecutive wins and fewest losses in any of the club's previous [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") seasons, as well as the fewest away defeats in any previous top-flight season. The record for consecutive victories came against [Watford](/wiki/Watford_F.C. "Watford F.C."), [Newcastle United](/wiki/Newcastle_United_F.C. "Newcastle United F.C."), [Crystal Palace](/wiki/Crystal_Palace_F.C. "Crystal Palace F.C."), [Southampton](/wiki/Southampton_F.C. "Southampton F.C.") and [Sunderland](/wiki/Sunderland_A.F.C. "Sunderland A.F.C."). Coincidentally, Leicester kept a record of five straight clean sheets against each of the same five opponents. The club's home crowds in 2015–16 saw their team beaten just once in the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") all season.[[186]](#cite_note-auto-187)

Leicester made their [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") debut in the [2016–17 season](/wiki/2016%E2%80%9317_UEFA_Champions_League "2016–17 UEFA Champions League"), their fourth appearance in European football. The club became the third English team to win on their Champions League debut, after Manchester United in 1994 and Newcastle United in 1997. They also became the first English team to win away on their Champions League debut, and win all three of their opening matches in the competition.[[197]](#cite_note-198)[[198]](#cite_note-199) Leicester are currently the first and only team in Champions League history to keep clean sheets in each of their opening four matches in the competition.[[199]](#cite_note-200) In March 2017, Leicester became the 50th team to reach the [UEFA Champions League](/wiki/UEFA_Champions_League "UEFA Champions League") quarter-finals.

During the start of the [2019–20 Premier League](/wiki/2019%E2%80%9320_Premier_League "2019–20 Premier League") campaign, the club won 12 matches from their opening 16 fixtures, which was the most victories recorded at this stage of any previous league season.[[200]](#cite_note-201)[[201]](#cite_note-auto3-202)[[202]](#cite_note-203)

On 25 October 2019, the Leicester team under manager Brendan Rodgers set the record for the highest margin of away victory in English top-flight history, defeating Southampton [9–0](/wiki/Southampton_F.C._0%E2%80%939_Leicester_City_F.C. "Southampton F.C. 0–9 Leicester City F.C.") at [St Mary's Stadium](/wiki/St_Mary%27s_Stadium "St Mary's Stadium"). In doing so, they also tied the record for the highest margin of victory in Premier League history, equalling Manchester United's [9–0 home victory](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C._9%E2%80%930_Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Manchester United F.C. 9–0 Ipswich Town F.C.") over [Ipswich Town](/wiki/Ipswich_Town_F.C. "Ipswich Town F.C.") in 1995.[[203]](#cite_note-204) As a result, Leicester City hold the all-time top tier records for the biggest defeat, biggest away win, and highest-scoring draw.

On 8 December 2019, a 1–4 away win at Aston Villa saw the club set a new record of eight consecutive victories. This surpassed the record of five consecutive wins during the [2015–16 Premier League](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Premier_League "2015–16 Premier League") title-winning campaign, and also surpassed Leicester's previous all-time top flight record of seven consecutive victories achieved between December 1962 and March 1963.[[201]](#cite_note-auto3-202)[[204]](#cite_note-205)

On 15 May 2021, Leicester City won the [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") for the first time in their history, having lost four previous finals in [1949](/wiki/1949_FA_Cup_final "1949 FA Cup final"), [1961](/wiki/1961_FA_Cup_final "1961 FA Cup final"), [1963](/wiki/1963_FA_Cup_final "1963 FA Cup final") and [1969](/wiki/1969_FA_Cup_final "1969 FA Cup final"). The club's record signing [Youri Tielemans](/wiki/Youri_Tielemans "Youri Tielemans") scored the winning goal against [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C "Chelsea F.C") at [Wembley Stadium](/wiki/Wembley_Stadium "Wembley Stadium").[[205]](#cite_note-206)

On 28 May 2023, the club was relegated from the Premier League with the highest ever squad value and player wages outside of the Premier League's traditional 'big-six' clubs.[[103]](#cite_note-Percy2024-103)[[102]](#cite_note-Hafez2023-102)

During the [2023–24 EFL Championship](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_EFL_Championship "2023–24 EFL Championship") season, the club set a number of new records. They made their best start to a league season to date, and the best in the competition's history (since becoming known as the Championship in 2004).[[206]](#cite_note-207) They also notably matched the all-time record of nine consecutive league wins home and away, and went four home matches without conceding for the first time since 1973.[[207]](#cite_note-208)[[208]](#cite_note-209)

## League history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leicester_City_F.C.&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: League history")]

Main article: [List of Leicester City F.C. seasons](/wiki/List_of_Leicester_City_F.C._seasons "List of Leicester City F.C. seasons")

Since their election to the [Football League](/wiki/Football_League "Football League") in 1894, Leicester City have spent all but one season within the top two tiers of English football. The club's longest stint at the top level to date came between 1957 and 1969. During these years, the club reached three FA Cup finals, participated in European football for the first time, and recorded their third highest ever league finish. During the [2008–09](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_in_English_football "2008–09 in English football") season, Leicester played in [League One](/wiki/Football_League_One "Football League One"), the third tier of English football, for the first time following the club's relegation from [the Championship](/wiki/Football_League_Championship "Football League Championship"). However, the club made an instant return to the second tier and were promoted as [2008–09 League One](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Football_League_One "2008–09 Football League One") champions.

## Honours

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Leicester_City_F.C.&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Honours")]

See also: [List of Leicester City F.C. records and statistics § Honours](/wiki/List_of_Leicester_City_F.C._records_and_statistics#Honours "List of Leicester City F.C. records and statistics")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/LCFC_lift_the_Premier_League_Trophy_%2826943755296%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-LCFC_lift_the_Premier_League_Trophy_%2826943755296%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:LCFC_lift_the_Premier_League_Trophy_(26943755296)_(cropped).jpg)

Leicester City players lifting the [2015–16 Premier League](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Premier_League "2015–16 Premier League") trophy.

Leicester City are currently one of five clubs, including [Manchester United](/wiki/Manchester_United_F.C. "Manchester United F.C."), [Manchester City](/wiki/Manchester_City_F.C. "Manchester City F.C."), [Chelsea](/wiki/Chelsea_F.C. "Chelsea F.C.") and [Liverpool](/wiki/Liverpool_F.C. "Liverpool F.C."), to have won the [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League"), [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup") and [League Cup](/wiki/EFL_Cup "EFL Cup") since 2000. Since the start of the millennium, they are the 6th most successful club in [English football](/wiki/English_football "English football") and one of 14 clubs to have won all four major domestic competitions.[[222]](#cite_note-223) The club also hold the record for the [most second division titles](/wiki/List_of_teams_promoted_from_the_English_Football_League_Championship_and_predecessors "List of teams promoted from the English Football League Championship and predecessors") with eight.[[223]](#cite_note-224)

**League**

* [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") / [Premier League](/wiki/Premier_League "Premier League") (level 1)
  + Champions: [2015–16](/wiki/2015%E2%80%9316_Premier_League "2015–16 Premier League")
  + Runners-up: [1928–29](/wiki/1928%E2%80%9329_Football_League "1928–29 Football League")
* [Second Division](/wiki/Football_League_Second_Division "Football League Second Division") / [First Division](/wiki/Football_League_First_Division "Football League First Division") / [Championship](/wiki/EFL_Championship "EFL Championship") (level 2)
  + Champions (8, record): [1924–25](/wiki/1924%E2%80%9325_Football_League#Second_Division "1924–25 Football League"), [1936–37](/wiki/1936%E2%80%9337_Football_League#Second_Division "1936–37 Football League"), [1953–54](/wiki/1953%E2%80%9354_Football_League#Second_Division "1953–54 Football League"), [1956–57](/wiki/1956%E2%80%9357_Football_League#Second_Division "1956–57 Football League"), [1970–71](/wiki/1970%E2%80%9371_Football_League#Second_Division "1970–71 Football League"), [1979–80](/wiki/1979%E2%80%9380_Football_League#Second_Division "1979–80 Football League"), [2013–14](/wiki/2013%E2%80%9314_Football_League_Championship "2013–14 Football League Championship"), [2023–24](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_EFL_Championship "2023–24 EFL Championship")
  + Runners-up: [1907–08](/wiki/1907%E2%80%9308_Football_League#Second_Division "1907–08 Football League"), [2002–03](/wiki/2002%E2%80%9303_Football_League#First_Division "2002–03 Football League")
  + Play-off winners: [1994](/wiki/1994_Football_League_play-offs#First_Division "1994 Football League play-offs"), [1996](/wiki/1996_Football_League_play-offs#First_Division "1996 Football League play-offs")
* [League One](/wiki/EFL_League_One "EFL League One") (level 3)
  + Champions: [2008–09](/wiki/2008%E2%80%9309_Football_League_One "2008–09 Football League One")

**Cup**

* [FA Cup](/wiki/FA_Cup "FA Cup")
  + Winners: [2020–21](/wiki/2020%E2%80%9321_FA_Cup "2020–21 FA Cup")
  + Runners-up: [1948–49](/wiki/1948%E2%80%9349_FA_Cup "1948–49 FA Cup"), [1960–61](/wiki/1960%E2%80%9361_FA_Cup "1960–61 FA Cup"), [1962–63](/wiki/1962%E2%80%9363_FA_Cup "1962–63 FA Cup"), [1968–69](/wiki/1968%E2%80%9369_FA_Cup "1968–69 FA Cup")
* [League Cup](/wiki/EFL_Cup "EFL Cup")
  + Winners: [1963–64](/wiki/1963%E2%80%9364_Football_League_Cup "1963–64 Football League Cup"), [1996–97](/wiki/1996%E2%80%9397_Football_League_Cup "1996–97 Football League Cup"), [1999–2000](/wiki/1999%E2%80%932000_Football_League_Cup "1999–2000 Football League Cup")
  + Runners-up: [1964–65](/wiki/1964%E2%80%9365_Football_League_Cup "1964–65 Football League Cup"), [1998–99](/wiki/1998%E2%80%9399_Football_League_Cup "1998–99 Football League Cup")
* [FA Charity Shield / FA Community Shield](/wiki/FA_Community_Shield "FA Community Shield")
  + Winners: [1971](/wiki/1971_FA_Charity_Shield "1971 FA Charity Shield"), [2021](/wiki/2021_FA_Community_Shield "2021 FA Community Shield")
  + Runners-up: [2016](/wiki/2016_FA_Community_Shield "2016 FA Community Shield")
